decomposition.title: RFP - Project Polaris: Evaluation of Hosting Options (Cloud, DCaaS & Hybrid)
decomposition.estimated_deal_size_usd: No explicit budget/value stated. Inferred future opportunity approximately USD $15M-$50M+ based on enterprise-scale hosting evaluation for transition away from a Wisconsin on-premises data center, covering large VMware/VxRail estate, significant physical server footprint, SAP ECC/BW/CRM/SCM/EWM/SRM/GRC/Fiori/BPC/Business Objects, multiple databases, backup/DR, networking, monitoring, security tooling and potential cloud/DCaaS/hybrid hosting. Phase 0 itself is only an evaluation/business-case RFP.
decomposition.contract_duration: No explicit contract term stated. The document describes a Phase 0 evaluation exercise under Project Polaris; detailed legal terms, contractual conditions, compliance requirements and SLAs will be finalized in subsequent phases following vendor selection.

requirements.text: Proposals must be submitted within the defined timeline via the designated communication channel.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Submission timing and channel requirements are often treated as non-responsive proposal controls.

requirements.text: Vendors are required to submit their responses using the Vendor Response template provided as part of this RFP to ensure consistency and comparability across all submissions.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: The mandatory response template is referenced as an associated document rather than fully embedded in the RFP body.

requirements.text: Vendors are required to complete the response template in detail; simple ‘Yes/No’ answers will not be considered sufficient.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: The document explicitly states simple Yes/No answers will not be sufficient, creating a non-compliance risk for abbreviated responses.

requirements.text: All communication related to this RFP should be directed to Nicholas.Groh@Kohler.com with copy to RFP-Responses-Polaris@kohler.com.
All clarification queries must be submitted within the defined timeline and use the template for Q&A provided to the contact provided.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Clarification queries have both a deadline and required Q&A template, which can be missed if only the timeline is reviewed.

requirements.text: Responses must be complete, clearly structured, and aligned to each requirement.
Vendors must clearly state all assumptions, dependencies, and constraints associated with their response.
Any optional services or additional capabilities not covered under the scope must be explicitly identified separately.
Vendors must provide high-level indicative solution designs and supporting explanations where relevant.
Vendors are requested to submit proposals detailing target architecture and platform capabilities.
Vendors are requested to submit proposals detailing a high-level transition approach and indicative sequencing.
Vendors are requested to submit proposals detailing an operating model and support framework.
Vendors are requested to submit proposals detailing commercial and pricing structure.
Proposed solutions should support enterprise-scale deployments, secure connectivity, and integration with Kohler’s enterprise and manufacturing ecosystem.
The evaluation should ensure application performance and availability are maintained or improved.
The evaluation should ensure integration with manufacturing and enterprise systems is supported.
The evaluation should ensure transition risks and business disruption are minimized.

Vendors are expected to propose a target operating model including roles and responsibilities/shared responsibility framework, governance/security/compliance approach, and operations and support model.
Proposed models should demonstrate how they can support a progressive transition away from the current Data Center footprint while maintaining business continuity and operational stability.
Vendors must clearly articulate the proposed solution covering target-state architecture aligned to multi-account / multi-subscription landing zone principles.
Vendors must clearly articulate the landing zone deployment model and application deployment model.
Vendors must clearly articulate environment segregation for Prod / Test / Dev.
Vendors must clearly articulate identity integration with enterprise identity providers.
Vendors must clearly articulate the hybrid connectivity approach.
Vendors must clearly articulate region strategy and data residency approach.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Data residency is a mandatory solution topic but the required residency rules are not specified in the main RFP.
requirements.jurisdiction_note: Region strategy and data residency approach are required, but no required data location is stated.

requirements.text: Vendors must clearly articulate disaster recovery, high availability and scalability patterns.
Architecture diagrams, reference designs and supporting documentation are strongly encouraged.
Vendors must respond for relevant scenarios (Cloud, DCaaS, Hybrid) where applicable and clearly state assumptions.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Scenario applicability may vary by supplier type, but vendors must clearly state assumptions.

requirements.text: Vendors are expected to base their response on the provided inventory dataset.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: The detailed inventory is an external attachment not included in the provided RFP text, but vendors are required to use it as baseline.

requirements.text: Vendors are expected to highlight any data gaps or assumptions made due to incomplete information.
Vendors are expected to use the provided inventory as the baseline for sizing, solution design, and indicative pricing.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: The inventory spreadsheet controls sizing and pricing but is not present in the provided document.

requirements.text: Where data gaps exist, vendors must clearly state assumptions used for sizing and costing.

Solutions must address the in-scope services including enterprise applications currently hosted in the Kohler Wisconsin Data Center, COTS and internally developed applications, Windows and Linux virtual machines, databases, middleware/integration platforms, file storage, backup and disaster recovery, identity and access management integrations, network connectivity, monitoring/logging/security tooling, and selected OT-adjacent workloads limited to read-only or controlled integration scenarios.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: The in-scope inventory is broad and includes enterprise, manufacturing-adjacent, SAP and infrastructure services; missing a workload class could make a response incomplete.

requirements.text: Vendors must factor out-of-scope execution items into consideration when the contract is in place, including detailed migration execution, application refactoring/remediation/modernization, low-level design, operational transition and steady-state managed services delivery commitments, execution of security controls/compliance certification/regulatory audits, tool implementation, training/change management, data cleansing/transformation/archival, and detailed testing execution.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: The RFP requires vendors to account for excluded execution activities when the contract is in place, creating scope and pricing boundary risk.

requirements.text: Vendors must demonstrate capability to host workloads in compliance with applicable industry/regional standards. Evidence of certifications required.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: This is a mandatory compliance evidence requirement with undefined certification list; failure to include evidence may make the response non-compliant.
requirements.jurisdiction_note: Applicable industry/regional standards are referenced but not specified.
requirements.cert_required: Evidence of certifications required; specific certifications not named in the provided document.

requirements.text: DCaaS providers must explicitly provide compliance attestations as per industry standards.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: This is a conditional DCaaS-only mandatory requirement buried in the compliance section and points to the Vendor Response Template for more information.
requirements.jurisdiction_note: Industry standards are referenced but not specified.
requirements.cert_required: DCaaS compliance attestations as per industry standards; specific attestations not named in the provided document.

requirements.text: Vendors are expected to ensure that their proposed solutions are capable of complying with applicable industry standards, regulatory requirements and data protection obligations relevant to enterprise IT and manufacturing environments.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Compliance obligations are restated in the appendix/legal section and may be missed if only the SOW is reviewed.
requirements.jurisdiction_note: Applicable industry standards, regulatory requirements and data protection obligations relevant to enterprise IT and manufacturing environments.

requirements.text: Pricing must be transparent, structured and clearly itemized, aligned to the requirements of this RFP and the inventory baseline provided.

Vendors must provide comprehensive cost breakup, including foundational setup costs, application and infrastructure hosting costs, migration/implementation costs if applicable, and any consulting, support, or managed services costs.
Vendors must provide separate cost views for SAP and non-SAP workloads, ensuring clear visibility into cost drivers across application categories.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Separate SAP and non-SAP pricing visibility is a specific pricing-format requirement that may be missed in a generic hosting cost model.

requirements.text: All commercial values and pricing submissions must be quoted in United States Dollars (USD).
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Currency format requirements can cause commercial non-compliance if pricing is submitted in another currency.

requirements.text: Proposals should include cost increase protections and efficiency gains that lead to a reduction or no more than a reasonable increase over time, based on prices being paid and not general discounts off list price.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: The RFP requires cost increase protections and efficiency gains based on actual paid prices, not merely discounts off list price.

requirements.text: Payment terms are an important part of the proposal; anything less than net 90 payment terms will not be considered competitive, and the proposal should contemplate no less than net 90 payment terms.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Net 90 is positioned as an important commercial expectation; shorter terms are explicitly stated to be non-competitive.

requirements.text: Vendors are expected to provide a high-level overview of their proposed program governance model aligned to the solution being proposed.

The governance overview should include indicative governance structure and engagement model, reporting and review mechanisms, escalation framework for issue resolution, and key roles and responsibilities at a high level.
Vendors must ensure that all information received from Kohler is used solely for the purpose of responding to this RFP.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: This legal/confidentiality obligation is in the appendix and restricts all use of Kohler information.

requirements.text: Vendors must not disclose, distribute, or share any information with third parties without prior written consent from Kohler.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: This appendix legal restriction may block use of subcontractors, offshore teams, hyperscaler partners, or other third parties unless Kohler gives prior written consent.

requirements.text: Vendors are expected to propose Cloud/DCaaS and/or Hybrid hosting solutions aligned to Kohler’s stated objectives and constraints.
Vendors are expected to review, validate, and clearly state any deviations or additional assumptions in their proposal.

ambiguities: Contract duration is not explicitly stated. The document says this is a Phase 0 evaluation and that detailed legal terms, contractual conditions, compliance requirements and SLAs will be defined in subsequent phases following vendor selection.

No explicit budget, estimated value, current annual spend, server counts, workload counts, storage volumes, or FTE counts are provided in the document. The document references a separate inventory baseline spreadsheet with counts and sizing, but it is not included in the provided text.
Key dates list day/month only; year is inferred as 2026 from the RFP cover date of 29-05-2026.
Detailed evaluation criteria, sub-categories, scoring structure and weightages are stated to be in the Evaluation Template Excel, but that template is not included in the provided document.

Required certifications are not named. The RFP states that evidence of certifications is required for applicable industry/regional standards, and DCaaS providers must provide compliance attestations, but specific standards are not listed.
The exact data residency obligations are not defined. Vendors must provide a region strategy and data residency approach, but no required geography or prohibited processing location is specified.

The RFP says execution of migration, implementation, security controls, operational transition and managed services delivery are out of scope, but pricing guidelines still ask for migration/implementation, consulting, support, and managed services costs if applicable.
The phrase 'designated communication channel' is not separately defined beyond the contact emails in Section 3.3.
The RFP is for evaluation and decision-making and does not constitute a commitment to a specific target architecture or execution approach.

similar_deals.why_relevant: Larger than Polaris but relevant for enterprise-scale manufacturing transformation where risk reduction, reusable IP and sector credibility influenced the award.
similar_deals.key_lesson: Proprietary IP reduced risk and strong sector credentials mattered. Accelerators and manufacturing proof points should be positioned as concrete de-risking assets.

similar_deals.title: Manufacturing IT Transformation
similar_deals.why_relevant: Relevant because Kohler is a manufacturing enterprise and Polaris will require trust in architecture, cost, performance and transition recommendations. No evidence of an incumbent Kohler relationship was provided, which is a risk.

similar_deals.title: Healthcare IT Transformation

win_intel.win_probability_rationale: Win probability is 41%. Evidence supports a credible but not advantaged bid. Positives: the past-proposal knowledge base contains a near-exact Polaris strategy with a strong 12-week vendor-neutral Hosting Decision Lab, open-book USD TCO, SAP/VMware/DCaaS/cloud/hybrid scope coverage and Phase 0 pricing patterns of USD $279K-$595K. The deal corpus shows relevant manufacturing transformation wins, especially DEAL-038 and DEAL-094, where sector credentials, proprietary IP and trust mattered, and DEAL-007/DEAL-031 patterns support using accelerators to reduce timeline and risk. Negatives: there is no public procurement data, so competitor pricing and incumbent strength cannot be validated; the strongest proposal match is WIP for this same RFP and not a proven win; Phase 0 evaluation work may favor an independent advisor, incumbent, hyperscaler-aligned provider or DCaaS provider; and comparable losses show real hazards, including DEAL-056 compliance failure and DEAL-043 overpricing. Because procurement ground truth is unavailable and no explicit Kohler incumbent relationship or prior win evidence was provided, confidence is medium-low and the probability is held below 50% despite good solution fit.
capability_gaps: Certification and attestation evidence is a live gap unless packaged before submission: the RFP requires vendors to demonstrate hosting compliance capability and provide evidence of certifications, and DCaaS providers must explicitly provide compliance attestations.

Vendor-neutrality must be made contractually credible: Phase 0 is an evaluation of cloud, DCaaS, retained data center and hybrid options, so any perceived bias toward our downstream migration/managed-services pull-through will weaken scoring unless addressed through a no-lock-in clause, conflict waiver, and Kohler-owned deliverables.

Manufacturing/SAP/VMware proof points must be specific, not generic: the RFP requires support for Kohler’s enterprise and manufacturing ecosystem and maintenance or improvement of application performance/availability; we need named or sanitized references for SAP hosting, VMware/VxRail modernization, data-center exit, backup/DR, plant/edge connectivity and hybrid operations.

Phase 0 pricing must be defensible and USD-only: the RFP requires commercial/pricing structure in USD, and deal-corpus losses show overpricing can lose even with a stronger solution; we need a fixed-fee advisory price with transparent assumptions, optional services separated, and downstream pricing clearly non-binding.

Submission compliance is a material execution gap unless tightly controlled: the RFP has eliminatory requirements for deadline, designated communication channel, mandatory Vendor Response template, and detailed answers rather than Yes/No responses.
recommended_win_themes: Vendor-neutral Hosting Decision Lab: position Polaris as a 12-week evidence-led decision factory comparing public cloud, DCaaS, retained Wisconsin data center, hybrid, plant/edge and SaaS integration workload by workload, rather than a biased cloud-first sales motion.
Open-book USD TCO and no-lock-in: emphasize transparent economics, assumptions, FinOps guardrails, scenario comparison and Kohler-owned outputs that can be competed downstream with any provider.

Manufacturing-resilient SAP and VMware modernization: focus on maintaining or improving performance and availability across SAP ECC/BW/CRM/SCM/EWM/SRM/GRC/Fiori/BPC/Business Objects, VMware/VxRail, physical servers, databases, backup/DR, network and plant/edge dependencies.

Risk-reduced transition roadmap: use proven accelerators for dependency mapping, workload placement scorecards, landing-zone blueprints, DR/security/observability designs, migration waves, rollback gates and vendor-coexistence RACI.

Compliance-first proposal execution: make procedural and certification compliance a visible differentiator, explicitly addressing template completeness, communication rules, USD currency, confidentiality, security certifications and DCaaS attestations.
risk_factors: No public procurement award data is available, so there is no ground-truth validation of likely Kohler competitors, incumbent advantage or market-clearing price.
The strongest past-proposal match is the same Kohler Project Polaris WIP record, not a proven win; it validates response strategy and pricing patterns but not buyer preference or awardability.

Phase 0 is evaluation-only, so Kohler may prefer a perceived independent advisor, incumbent infrastructure provider, hyperscaler partner, or DCaaS provider over a transformation-led SI unless vendor neutrality is proven.

Comparable deal corpus is mixed: manufacturing transformation wins exist, but DEAL-056 was lost due to a compliance miss and DEAL-043 shows that being 20% above the winning bid can lose despite a better solution.

Strict eliminatory instructions create avoidable bid risk: deadline, designated channel, response template, detailed responses, USD currency, confidentiality and third-party disclosure rules must all be followed exactly.

Unknown incumbent relationship with Kohler lowers confidence; DEAL-094 shows pre-existing client relationship can be decisive in large manufacturing IT awards, and no evidence was provided that we have that advantage here.
must_fix_before_bidding: Create a red-team compliance checklist covering due date, submission channel, required email recipients, Q&A template, Vendor Response template completion, USD-only pricing, confidentiality restrictions, and prohibition on third-party disclosure without consent.
Attach a certification and compliance evidence pack covering our hosting/security certifications plus explicit DCaaS partner attestations if any DCaaS option is included.
Price Phase 0 as a competitive fixed-fee advisory engagement, ideally within the historically supported USD $279K–$595K range, with optional add-ons and downstream execution clearly separated.
Include a contractual no-lock-in / Kohler-owned-output commitment so the Phase 0 decision package can be used with any downstream provider.

Add manufacturing-relevant proof points and accelerators: SAP dependency mapping, VMware/VxRail discovery, DC-exit runbooks, TCO/FinOps model, DR tiering, migration-wave templates, and U.S. or global manufacturing references.

competitors.likelihood_to_bid: high; confidence high. The Waukesha, WI job posting for application migration assessment, migration strategy, execution, testing, decommissioning and hypercare is directly aligned to this RFP geography and skill set. Recent manufacturing/global IT wins with SKF, Elopak and Nokian Tyres reinforce pursuit relevance. [Job posting: LinkedIn Waukesha role; Web: TCS FY27 Q1, SKF, Elopak, Nokian Tyres]
competitors.predicted_positioning: TCS will likely position as the low-risk global transformation factory for SAP + infrastructure + cloud + AI-led operations: 'use TCS scale, Enterprise Cloud, SAP partnership, AI/Cognix, zero-trust and manufacturing experience to build a business case and then execute migration/run.' Evidence: Q1 FY27 revenue $7.624B, operating margin 24.0%, TCV $9.5B, SKF $800M mega-deal, Elopak global IT operations deal, Nokian Tyres AI-led modernization, and Waukesha migration hiring. [Web: TCS Q1 FY27 results and cited releases; Job posting: LinkedIn]
competitors.predicted_price_range_usd: Phase 0 evaluation estimate: $400,000-$650,000; confidence medium. Calculation: TCS Q1 FY27 revenue $7.624B x 4 = $30.496B annualized; workforce 593,798; revenue/employee = about $51,360/year. For an 8-12 FTE-year blended assessment/migration-planning effort implied by SAP, VMware/VxRail, physical servers, DB, backup/DR, network/security scope, revenue/employee anchor gives $410,880-$616,320. With 24.0% operating margin, implied cost floor = price x 0.76 = $312,269-$468,403, and price floor math = cost/(1-0.24). No public contract award for this Wisconsin opportunity was provided. [Financial: TCS Q1 FY27 results; Source 2: no public procurement data]
their_strengths: Strongest local bid signal among the set: Waukesha, WI job posting for cloud/application migration assessment and hypercare. [Job posting: LinkedIn]
Large profitable delivery engine: $7.624B Q1 FY27 revenue, 24.0% operating margin, $9.5B Q1 TCV and 593,798 workforce. [Financial/Web: TCS Q1 FY27 results]
Recent manufacturing/global IT references: SKF $800M mega-deal, Nokian Tyres AI-led modernization and Elopak global IT operations transformation. [Web: TCS releases]
Documented SAP, hybrid cloud, Enterprise Cloud, SAP HANA on cloud, zero-trust and multicloud capabilities. [Web: TCS Enterprise Cloud, SAP partnership, IDC multicloud materials]
their_weaknesses: No recent public contract wins in Wisconsin were provided; Source 2 says no public procurement data available.
Waukesha hiring is a signal, not proof of Kohler account pursuit; the source does not tie the role to this RFP. [Job posting: LinkedIn]

TCS's likely proposal may lean heavily on broad AI-led transformation language; the cited SKF/Elopak/Nokian evidence supports global modernization, not specific Kohler DCaaS compliance attestations. [Web: TCS releases]
Attrition remains material at 13.6% LTM IT services attrition, so named-team retention and continuity should be challenged. [Financial/Web: TCS Q1 FY27 results]
how_to_beat_them: Make the bid a Wisconsin-led, named-team Phase 0 with guaranteed onsite discovery workshops. TCS has a strong Waukesha signal, but only one cited local role: a LinkedIn Waukesha, WI Software Developer posting covering OCI, AWS ECS/EKS/S3, application migration assessment, migration strategy, decommissioning and hypercare; there is no public evidence tying that role to Kohler. [Job posting: LinkedIn Waukesha, WI role]

Neutralize TCS's AI-led modernization story by requiring RFP-template-level evidence for VMware/VxRail disposition, SAP ECC/BW/CRM/SCM/EWM/SRM/GRC/Fiori/BPC/BOBJ hosting options, DR, backup, network and security controls rather than generic Cognix/AI claims. Their recent wins with SKF, Elopak and Nokian Tyres prove AI-led global IT operations positioning, not a Wisconsin DCaaS award. [Web: TCS Q1 FY27; SKF; Elopak; Nokian Tyres releases]

Exploit compliance and independence: require DCaaS compliance attestations, USD pricing, and a vendor-neutral option scorecard that does not bias toward TCS-run managed services. No recent public contract wins in Wisconsin were provided; Source 2 states no public procurement data available.

Price just below their likely comfort band with a fixed-fee Phase 0, because TCS's 24.0% operating margin creates a high profit expectation even though its offshore-heavy revenue/employee is low. [Financial: TCS Q1 FY27 results]

competitors.likelihood_to_bid: high; confidence high. Accenture has a Wisconsin/Milwaukee cloud architecture job signal and multiple U.S. SAP/cloud/security roles aligned to the RFP. It also has recent manufacturing, OT security, SAP and cloud modernization market activity. [Job postings: Accenture Milwaukee Cloud Solution Architect for D365, SAP Architect, SAP Basis Admin, SAP Security; Web/Financial: Q3 FY2026 results and 2026 releases]

draft.executive_summary:
Project Polaris is not a binary cloud-versus-data-center exercise. It is a resilience, cost, architecture, security and operating-model decision for Kohler, where Wisconsin data-center choices, global manufacturing continuity, SAP performance, digital platforms and customer trust must continue to perform while options are evaluated.

The client intelligence points to a clear mandate. In August 2025, The Org identified CTO priorities around using digital and data to simplify processes, deepen engagement and spark innovation. In January 2026, Cognizant linked Kohler technology priorities to operational and business excellence, productivity and customer value; its 2026 material also points to streamlining enterprise applications and advancing the cloud journey for innovation and growth.

We propose a 12-week, vendor-neutral Polaris Hosting Decision Lab: an evidence-led decision factory comparing public cloud, DCaaS, retained Wisconsin data center, hybrid, plant/edge and SaaS integration workload by workload. The outputs will be Kohler-owned: target-state architecture, dependency maps, SAP and VMware disposition scorecards, landing-zone blueprints, DR tiers, security evidence matrix, migration waves, rollback gates, vendor-coexistence RACI, open-book USD TCO and FinOps controls.

Our distinctive commitment is contractual independence. We will not bid the Phase 1 hosting, migration or run-services contract unless Kohler explicitly waives that restriction after receiving the final business case. Polaris becomes a conflict-free decision package, not a sales funnel, and a governed foundation for innovation, reliability and growth.

sections.section_title: Target state architecture, security and governance
sections.content:
The target state will be designed as a governed hybrid-hosting strategy, not a preselected cloud destination. We will baseline Kohler workloads, then compare placement across public cloud, DCaaS, retained Wisconsin data center, hybrid, plant/edge and SaaS integration using architecture, risk, performance, compliance and cost criteria.

The architecture will define landing-zone patterns, account and subscription structure, identity and privileged access, network segmentation, encryption, key management, logging, policy-as-code, backup integration and shared-responsibility controls. SAP ECC/BW/CRM/SCM/EWM/SRM/GRC/Fiori/BPC/Business Objects, VMware/VxRail, physical servers, databases and manufacturing edge dependencies will receive explicit disposition scorecards rather than generic migration tags.

Governance will be run through an architecture review board, security evidence matrix, decision register and Kohler-owned standards library. This advances the Vendor-neutral Hosting Decision Lab theme by giving Kohler a defensible target state that any downstream provider can implement without lock-in.

The transition strategy starts with evidence, not movement. Phase 0 will build the workload inventory, dependency graph, business criticality model, RTO/RPO profile, security constraints, license considerations and incumbent-service touchpoints before any migration recommendation is made.

We will organize candidates into waves by dependency, risk and business value: quick-win SaaS integrations, modernization-ready virtual workloads, SAP-dependent systems requiring performance validation, plant/edge workloads requiring local resilience, and retained workloads that should remain in DCaaS or Wisconsin facilities. Each wave will include readiness gates, migration rehearsal requirements, rollback plans, data validation, user acceptance, ServiceNow change records and vendor-coexistence RACI.

Incumbent transition is treated as a workstream, not an afterthought. We will define knowledge transfer, access handoff, dual-run periods, cutover decision rights and issue escalation paths. The result is a risk-reduced transition roadmap that protects operations while preparing Kohler to compete Phase 1 with confidence.

Kohler's hosting decision will depend on dependable connectivity between plants, offices, cloud regions, DCaaS facilities, retained Wisconsin assets, SaaS platforms and security services. We will therefore assess the network as a decision criterion, not a downstream implementation detail.

The Polaris network workstream will baseline WAN patterns, latency-sensitive SAP and manufacturing traffic, DNS, IP addressing, routing, firewalls, load balancing, remote access, third-party connectivity, internet egress, OT/IT segmentation and plant/edge dependencies. Target designs will compare direct cloud connectivity, DCaaS interconnects, SD-WAN options, segmented transit, zero-trust access and resilient paths for critical users and sites.

Connectivity scenarios will be costed in the open-book USD TCO model, including bandwidth, circuits, managed network services and redundancy assumptions. This supports the no-lock-in theme by making network choices transparent, portable and ready for provider competition.

Availability and resilience will be evaluated by workload criticality, not by a single platform SLA. We will establish service baselines for SAP, VMware/VxRail, databases, physical servers, backup, collaboration dependencies, plant-facing applications and digital customer platforms, then map each workload to target RTO, RPO, recovery tier and operational owner.

The assessment will compare active-active, active-passive, backup-and-restore, pilot-light, DCaaS continuity and retained data-center options. Designs will include backup immutability, recovery orchestration, dependency-aware restart order, DR testing cadence, monitoring coverage, incident escalation and evidence required to prove recoverability. SAP and manufacturing workloads will receive specific performance and cutover gates before any transition recommendation.

AIOps-ready observability will be built into the resilience design, using event correlation, service dashboards and reliability signals to improve agility. This reinforces manufacturing-resilient modernization: Kohler gains a practical DR roadmap that protects production, customers and digital trust.

Polaris will provide open-book USD economics that leadership can audit, challenge and reuse. We propose a 12-week fixed-price Phase 0 advisory package at USD 279,533, aligned to the prior Kohler hosting-decision benchmark in the knowledge base, with assumptions separated from optional downstream scope.

The TCO model will compare public cloud, DCaaS, retained Wisconsin data center, hybrid, plant/edge and SaaS integration scenarios over agreed planning horizons. It will show compute, storage, database, backup, network, security, observability, licensing, labor, transition, dual-run, decommissioning, DR and managed-service assumptions. FinOps guardrails will define tagging, budget controls, variance thresholds, showback, optimization cadence and decision rights.

Our no-follow-on-bid covenant removes the economic conflict that often turns advisory into a sales wedge. Kohler receives a no-lock-in business case and commercial package that can be competed downstream on transparent terms.

The operating model will show how each hosting option is governed, supported and improved after the decision. We will map current responsibilities across Kohler, incumbents, cloud providers, DCaaS providers, network carriers, security operations and application teams, then define future-state RACI options that make ownership explicit.

Support designs will cover ITIL processes, ServiceNow integration, incident and problem management, change governance, access management, patching, backup operations, monitoring response, security escalation, major incident communications, vendor management and executive reporting. We will define 24/7 support expectations for critical SAP, infrastructure, plant/edge and digital platform services while separating advisory recommendations from any downstream run-services bid.

The model will include transition knowledge transfer, dual-run support, hypercare criteria and steady-state performance dashboards. This advances the risk-reduced transition theme by giving Kohler an operating model that can absorb change without losing accountability.

The toolset will be selected to produce evidence, accelerate analysis and leave Kohler with reusable assets. We will use discovery and dependency-mapping accelerators, workload placement scorecards, landing-zone blueprint templates, migration-wave planners, TCO models, risk registers, security evidence matrices, DR tiering models and RACI templates proven in manufacturing hosting and platform-modernization pursuits.

The ecosystem assessment will compare provider maturity without bias toward a preferred destination. Public cloud, DCaaS, retained data center, VMware modernization, backup, database, network, observability, AIOps, security, SaaS integration and plant/edge options will be scored against Kohler criteria. Where provider attestations or certifications are required, they will be captured as evidence rather than sales claims.

Outputs will be editable, Kohler-owned and suitable for Phase 1 competition. This makes tools and ecosystem maturity part of the Vendor-neutral Hosting Decision Lab, not a black-box assessment.

Our deliverable set is structured to match the RFP evaluation criteria and give Kohler a complete decision package, not fragments that require rework. Phase 0 will produce the current-state baseline, application and infrastructure inventory, dependency maps, workload placement matrix, target-state architecture, security and governance blueprint, network and connectivity design, resilience and DR model, migration roadmap, operating model, risk register and executive decision deck.

Commercial deliverables will include open-book USD TCO, FinOps guardrails, scenario assumptions, sensitivity analysis, provider-comparison criteria and downstream sourcing package. Transition deliverables will include migration waves, rollback gates, test evidence requirements, cutover approach, incumbent coexistence RACI, communications plan and hypercare entry and exit criteria.

We will also provide a compliance response matrix covering template completeness, confidentiality, communication rules, security certifications and DCaaS attestations. This closes with the compliance-first execution theme: Kohler receives evaluation-ready materials that support governance, competition and accountable action.

critical_issues: The recommended price of $30,667,392 is supported in the knowledge base only as a 36-month downstream hybrid modernization / managed-services program. The raw knowledge base explicitly says to use the $279K-$595K range for Phase 0 if the buyer is asking only for evaluation/business case work, so presenting $30.667M as the main recommended bid price for Phase 0 is materially misaligned.

Competitor pricing calculations are not supported by the provided financial data. The raw SEC/financial data says TCS financial data is unavailable and Accenture financial data is unavailable, and provides no Kyndryl, Deloitte, DXC, or HCLTech financials. Revenue, workforce, operating margin, and revenue-per-employee calculations for those firms should not be treated as verified.

The competitor likelihood and confidence ratings rely heavily on unsupported external claims: job postings, recent wins, acquisitions, GSA awards, SAP/cloud releases, Kyndryl large-deal counts, and HCLTech deal values are not present in the raw data sources.

The claimed Deloitte & Touche LLP Treasury/FinCEN Infrastructure & Cloud Platform USAspending award at $3.266M is not present in the provided USAspending ground-truth list and should not be used as a public-award pricing anchor.

The win-probability rationale cites DEAL-038, DEAL-094, DEAL-007, DEAL-031, DEAL-056, and DEAL-043. None of those deal-corpus records or outcomes appear in the raw data, so the supporting evidence for comparable wins/losses is unverified.

The killer differentiator is directionally consistent with the knowledge base's vendor-neutral/no-lock-in theme, but the stronger assertion that none of TCS, Accenture, Kyndryl, Deloitte, DXC, or HCLTech is structurally positioned to match it is unsupported by the provided sources.

The no-follow-on-bid covenant is a plausible extension of the knowledge-base strategy but is not directly stated in the raw data. It should be positioned as a proposed enhancement, not as verified prior strategy.

Several claimed RFP requirements are not present in the supplied raw data, including mandatory Vendor Response template, eliminatory deadline/channel rules, and explicit DCaaS compliance-attestation requirements.

The raw procurement data contains unrelated UK and U.S. public contracts and no Kohler/Wisconsin procurement award. Therefore incumbent strength, competitor pricing, and public-award comparables for this opportunity cannot be validated.
removed_claims: Remove or reframe the $30,667,392 recommended price as an optional downstream 36-month program price, not the Phase 0 bid price.
Remove the TCS Q1 FY27 revenue, workforce, revenue-per-employee, 24.0% operating margin, and derived $400K-$650K price calculation as verified claims.
Remove the Accenture Q3 FY2026 revenue, workforce, revenue-per-employee, 17.0% operating margin, and derived $800K-$1.2M price calculation as verified claims.
Remove the Kyndryl FY2026 operating margin, U.S.-segment large-deal activity, Bridge positioning, job-posting evidence, and $650K-$1.3M price calculation as verified claims.
Remove the Deloitte Treasury/FinCEN $3.266M USAspending award, Deloitte Netherlands margin, Deloitte job postings, and $1.2M-$2.8M price anchor as verified claims.
Remove the DXC Q4 FY2026 adjusted EBIT margin, GAAP EBIT margin, GSA MAS posture, job-posting evidence, and $500K-$950K price calculation as verified claims.
Remove the HCLTech FY26 revenue, workforce, EBIT margin, U.S. electronics manufacturer win, U.S. SAP modernization wins, Milwaukee hiring signal, and $500K-$800K price calculation as verified claims.
Remove competitor likelihood labels of 'high' or 'medium-high' where they are based on unsupported financials, hiring signals, or external wins.

Remove references to TCS SKF, Elopak, and Nokian Tyres wins; Accenture Cloud First/SAP/managed-services bookings and acquisitions; Kyndryl 38 contracts over $50M; DXC SAP/Microsoft/ServiceNow positioning; and HCLTech $1.14B managed workplace/network deal unless external source evidence is added.
Remove DEAL-038, DEAL-094, DEAL-007, DEAL-031, DEAL-056, and DEAL-043 as evidence unless the underlying deal-corpus records are supplied.
Remove the statement that the strongest proposal match is WIP for this same RFP; the raw knowledge base shows Kohler Polaris proposal/intelligence records but does not verify WIP status.
Remove the claim that none of the six competitors is structurally positioned to match the no-follow-on-bid covenant.
Remove the claim that the RFP has eliminatory deadline/channel/template requirements unless the actual RFP text is provided.
Remove the claim that DCaaS providers must explicitly provide compliance attestations unless the actual RFP text is provided.
warnings: Use the knowledge-base Phase 0 pricing range of $279,533 to $595,000 for an evaluation/business-case-only proposal; reserve $24.75M-$35.7M, including $30.667M, for optional downstream transformation scenarios.
The only financial data supplied is for Infosys, which is not one of the listed competitors; all named-competitor financial metrics are unsupported by the provided ground truth.
No cloud pricing data was provided, so infrastructure TCO or cloud/DCaaS price claims cannot be independently validated.
The win probability should be reduced because key supporting evidence comes from unsupported deal IDs and speculative competitor analysis, even though the Kohler Polaris knowledge-base fit is strong.
If competitor analysis is retained, it should be labeled as speculative market reasoning with low confidence, not evidence-backed pricing intelligence.

pursuit_plan.strategy: Pursue this as a strategic Phase 0 foothold into a much larger data-center exit and managed hosting transformation. Lead with an objective, evidence-backed decision framework comparing cloud, DCaaS, and hybrid options for Kohler's SAP-heavy, VMware-heavy, manufacturing-integrated estate, while demonstrating that we can safely carry the chosen option into migration and operations. The proposal should win trust through neutrality, compliance discipline, transparent economics, and a practical transition roadmap that protects manufacturing continuity.
pursuit_plan.win_strategy: Win by being the most objective and commercially transparent advisor for Kohler's hosting decision, proving we can compare cloud, DCaaS, and hybrid without bias while de-risking SAP/manufacturing continuity and creating a credible path from Phase 0 business case to future execution.
pursuit_plan.pricing_approach: Use a hybrid pricing approach: fixed-fee or capped T&M for the Phase 0 evaluation, combined with transparent value-based/TCO modeling for the future hosting options. Quote everything in USD, separate mandatory Phase 0 deliverables from optional migration, implementation, managed services, support, security, connectivity, backup/DR, and tooling work. Because evaluation weights are not provided and cost transparency is explicitly scored, avoid opaque bundled pricing; show scenario-based economics for public cloud, DCaaS, and hybrid with assumptions, sensitivity ranges, one-time versus recurring costs, and clear commercial levers such as reserved capacity, software licensing treatment, migration factory scale, support tiers, and contract-term discounts.
key_risks: Phase 0 is an evaluation only; overinvesting in a full migration proposal could miss the requested decision-support scope, while underinvesting could weaken credibility for the future USD $15M-$50M+ execution opportunity.

Eight eliminatory requirements create avoidable disqualification risk, especially mandatory template use, detailed non-Yes/No responses, certification evidence, DCaaS attestations, USD pricing, and strict submission channel/timeline compliance.
The inventory baseline spreadsheet is not included, so sizing, current-state cost, workload criticality, storage, network, server counts, FTEs, and pricing accuracy are uncertain.

Evaluation weights are missing, so the response must be balanced across architecture, migration, networking, DR, cost transparency, operating model, tools/platform maturity, and deliverable alignment rather than over-optimizing for one dimension.
SAP complexity is high: ECC, BW, CRM, SCM, EWM, SRM, GRC, Fiori, BPC, and Business Objects may have latency, licensing, OS/database, supportability, performance, and future S/4HANA implications.

Manufacturing ecosystem integration may include plant-floor dependencies, latency-sensitive systems, regional connectivity, downtime constraints, and non-standard physical server workloads not suitable for simple lift-and-shift.
Data residency and required certifications are unspecified, creating risk that proposed regions, support locations, or third-party platforms may not satisfy Kohler's eventual compliance expectations.
DCaaS versus cloud comparison could be biased by vendor business model; Kohler likely values an objective, transparent options analysis, so perceived lack of neutrality is a win/loss risk.
Pricing must include enough future migration, support, and managed-services economics to support a business case, even though implementation and run services are formally out of Phase 0 scope.
Third-party disclosure restrictions may limit use of subcontractors, hyperscaler solution reviews, offshore teams, or external benchmark providers unless prior written consent is obtained.

gaps: Competitor intelligence appears materially incomplete: the provided output only shows TCS, while the strongest competitive threat is likely Cognizant as incumbent/renewed partner for cloud, data center operations, cyber, enterprise systems and AI operations.

Competitor pricing is only moderately grounded. TCS Phase 0 price is derived from corporate revenue-per-employee and margin math, not from advisory/assessment rate cards, comparable infrastructure assessment deals, or known cloud/DCaaS business-case pricing.

Competitor vulnerabilities are somewhat specific for TCS, but the set is not broad enough to support a competitive strategy. Missing likely bidders include Cognizant, Kyndryl, HCLTech, Infosys, Wipro, Accenture, Deloitte, Rackspace, NTT, DXC, Ensono, and hyperscaler-aligned cloud partners.
Win intel has a weak first comparable: it uses the same Kohler/Project Polaris pursuit record as a similar deal, which is useful for reuse but not valid evidence of win probability.
Win probability calibration is not visible in the provided WIN_INTEL excerpt. Similar deals are helpful but include mixed relevance and at least one truncated/lost-deal record.
Client intel is reasonably strong, but several cited signals are from 2025/2026 web sources and should be treated as directional unless source links/details are retained for proposal substantiation.
strengths: Client intel contains specific, actionable signals tied to Kohler’s recent digital, cloud, infrastructure, cyber and AI priorities.
Unstated needs are not generic; hybrid operating model, incumbent transition, AIOps, security-by-design, FinOps and workload-placement business case are relevant to the Polaris scope.
RFP compliance/disqualifier risks are well captured and should materially improve submission discipline.
TCS analysis includes some specific signals, including a Waukesha migration-related hiring indicator and relevant manufacturing/global IT references.
Win intel surfaces useful historical lessons around compliance rigor, sector proof points, reusable IP and the importance of trust/buyer intimacy.

agentic_decisions.reasoning: Pursue this as a strategic Phase 0 foothold into a much larger data-center exit and managed hosting transformation. Lead with an objective, evidence-backed decision framework comparing cloud, DCaaS, and hybrid options for Kohler's SAP-heavy, VMware-heavy, manufacturing-integrated estate, while demonstrating that we can safely carry the chosen option into migration and operations. The proposal should win trust through neutrality, compliance discipline, transparent economics, and a practical transition roadmap that protects manufacturing continuity.

The client intelligence is strong enough to proceed: it identifies specific Kohler signals around Cognizant renewal, CDO priorities, data/AI, cybersecurity, cloud journey, and hybrid operations. The win intel is partially useful because it surfaces manufacturing transformation lessons and compliance risks, but it overweights the same WIP pursuit as a comparable and does not clearly show a calibrated win probability. The main blocker is competitor intelligence: for this RFP, ignoring or underdeveloping Cognizant would materially hurt the proposal because Cognizant appears to be the incumbent across exactly the scope Polaris evaluates. A TCS-only or TCS-heavy competitive view risks building the wrong win themes, pricing anchors and displacement strategy. This is a material proposal risk, not a minor gap.
